title: [Bug]: Subject-aware observability certification helper trips Bandit on synthetic HEC token env name
summary: The subject-aware observability certification helper used a synthetic Splunk HEC environment variable name that contained the word token, which made Bandit treat the test marker as a possible hardcoded secret.
observed: The full local check stopped during the Bandit security scan for the subject-aware observability certification helper.
expected: Certification helpers should avoid synthetic strings that look like secret values while still exercising the same validated connector policy paths.
reproduction: Run scripts/check.sh on the issue branch after adding the subject-aware observability certification helper.
impact: The issue blocks release validation for the new certification helper but does not expose a real secret or affect released runtime behavior.
delivery_semantics: The fix is limited to a certification-helper test marker and must not change ACK ordering, retries, DLQ publication, sink writes, or idempotency behavior.
security: The fix keeps security scanning strict and prevents public evidence from containing strings that resemble token material, credentials, payloads, or operational identifiers.
acceptance: The Bandit security scan passes without suppressing the rule.
The subject-aware observability certification helper still validates Splunk HEC connector output.
The focused subject-aware observability certification tests continue to pass.
tests: Run the focused subject-aware observability certification suite and scripts/check.sh.
documentation: No user-facing documentation change is required beyond the issue #127 certification documentation already being added.
closeout: Close only after the release containing the fix is published with sanitized test evidence.
